coredata.dc:title: Development of a rapid and ultrasensitive acrylamide nanosensor based on TiO2 NPs/GQDs nanocomposite
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Aafria, Shatrughan
Sharma, Minakshi
coredata.dc:description: Acrylamide (AA), a well-known human carcinogen, is a toxic compound formed in thermally processed foods via the Millard reaction. This study summarizes a nanosensor-based rapid detection of AA. Pencil graphite (PG) electrode modified with Titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs)/ Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) nanocomposite and Hemoglobin (Hb) have been prepared for application as stable and sensitive AA nanosensor. The synthesized nanoparticles were characterized by Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–visible spectroscopy, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD), and Zeta potential analysis. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), FTIR, and electrochemical Impedance (EIS) measurements were performed to characterize the working electrode at every modification stage. The optimum response was observed at 30ᵒC in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). The developed nanosensor showed a wide linear range of 10 nM to 200 nM with a very low detection limit of 0.67 nM, low quantification limit (LOQ) of 2.23 nM, and a very high sensitivity of 2.40 mAcm−2nM−1. The suggested strategy used less expensive materials to create a compelling and environmentally sustainable detection process. With the use of this innovative nanocomposite material, biosensing platforms for the on-site, ultrasensitive, and selective detection of different harmful chemicals in food safety studies can be developed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acrylamide
Nanocomposite
Nanosensor
Graphene quantum dots
Titanium dioxide nanoparticles
Processed food

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrodeposition of TiO2 NPs/GQDs nanocomposite onto PG electrode
Detection of AA in processed foods
Performance and applications of the developed nanosensor
Safety guidelines for handling AA in the laboratory

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Molecular docking with Autodock Vina showing the interaction of AA with β- globulin chain of Hb.
(A) UV–visible spectrum, (B) Surface zeta potential, (C) FTIR spectrum, and (D)TEM images of GQDs.
(A) surface zeta potential, (B) UV–visible spectrum, (C) FTIR spectrum, (D) TEM images, (E) XRD powder pattern of TiO2 NPs.

FTIR spectrum of nanocomposite modified electrode(A), and Hb immobilized modified electrode (B), SEM images of bare PG electrode (C), TiO2 NPs/GQDs/PG electrode (D), and Hb/TiO2 NPs/GQDs/GQDs (E), Impedance spectra of modified PG electrode in 5 mM K3Fe(CN)6:K4Fe(CN)6 (1:1) (F), and Cyclic voltammogram of the working PG electrode at different stages of its preparation, showing a difference in the current in case of bare PG electrode (black), TiO2 NPs/GQDs modified PG electrode (red), Hb/ TiO2 NPs/GQDs/PG electrode (orange) in 5 mM ferricyanide [K3Fe(CN)6] solution prepared in 1.0 M KCl (G).

(A) Effect of pH on nanosensor’s response, (B) effect of temperature on nanosensor’s response, (C) Response measurement of the developed electrode in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0), containing 100 μl of 1.0 nM AA at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1. Current decreases after the addition of AA (green) as compared to unbound Hb/TiO2 NPs/GQDs/PG electrode (blue), suggesting the formation of AA-Hb adduct, (D) An inverse linear curve representing the relationship between the substrate (AA) concentration and response measurement (in current).

(A) Interference study with propionic acid (PA), ascorbic acid (Aa), acetate, tartrate, and acrylic acid, each having 1 μM concentration added to 100 nM of AA into 30 mL phosphate buffer (pH 7.0), (B) storage -stability performance of the Hb/TiO2 NPs/GQDs/PG nanosensor.
Analytical recoveries of constructed AA nanosensor.
A comparative study of different electrochemical sensors for AA detection.

row.entry: Potato chips, biscuits, bread, fried nuts

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of Hb/TiO2 NPs/GQDs/PG nanosensor for AA detection in various food samples.

head.ce:title: Development of a rapid and ultrasensitive acrylamide nanosensor based on TiO2 NPs/GQDs nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Zoology, Maharshi Dayanand University, Rohtak, Haryana 124001, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Zoology, Maharshi Dayanand University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Zoology, Maharshi Dayanand University, Rohtak 124001, Haryana India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acrylamide (AA), a well-known human carcinogen, is a toxic compound formed in thermally processed foods via the Millard reaction. This study summarizes a nanosensor-based rapid detection of AA. Pencil graphite (PG) electrode modified with Titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs)/ Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) nanocomposite and Hemoglobin (Hb) have been prepared for application as stable and sensitive AA nanosensor. The synthesized nanoparticles were characterized by Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–visible spectroscopy, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD), and Zeta potential analysis. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), FTIR, and electrochemical Impedance (EIS) measurements were performed to characterize the working electrode at every modification stage. The optimum response was observed at 30ᵒC in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). The developed nanosensor showed a wide linear range of 10 nM to 200 nM with a very low detection limit of 0.67 nM, low quantification limit (LOQ) of 2.23 nM, and a very high sensitivity of 2.40 mAcm−2nM−1. The suggested strategy used less expensive materials to create a compelling and environmentally sustainable detection process. With the use of this innovative nanocomposite material, biosensing platforms for the on-site, ultrasensitive, and selective detection of different harmful chemicals in food safety studies can be developed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The potential health dangers associated with AA have led to an increase in concerns regarding its prevalence in food products in recent years. The International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) has designated AA as a group 2 A carcinogen (Pan et al., 2020). It is a chemical molecule generated during food processing at high temperatures (>120ᵒC) and low moisture conditions. Baking, roasting, and frying are the high-temperature cooking methods that naturally produce AA in foods with high starch content (Baskar and Aiswarya, 2018). It is produced by the Maillard reaction between amino acid asparagine and reducing sugars (Rannou et al., 2016).

AA can be quickly absorbed by the body via digestive, respiratory, and dermal routes and distributed to various tissues, including the liver, thymus, heart, brain, and placenta, through the circulatory system (Semla et al., 2017). AA interacts with DNA and proteins, resulting in the formation of adducts and changing their structure and functionality. This alteration may prevent proteins from operating normally (Rifai and Saleh, 2020). AA may be converted to glycidamide by the catalysis of the cytochrome P450 enzyme. It has been demonstrated that AA and its metabolite glycidamide have carcinogenic and neurotoxic properties (Obón-Santacana et al., 2016). They may disrupt nerve cell activity, disrupt nerve cell function, and cause neurological diseases and disabilities. The glycidamide metabolite has a stronger reactivity with proteins and DNA than the AA molecule. It creates DNA adducts and promotes the growth of rodent tumors (Rifai and Saleh, 2020). The neurological system is a major target for AA's harmful effects as the peripheral nerve and nerve terminals deteriorate in some brain areas, including the cerebral cortex, hypothalamus, and hippocampus. Frequent exposure to AA is linked to memory, learning, and cognitive dysfunction (Matoso et al., 2019). Humans have experienced damage to their CNS and PNS because of prolonged exposure to AA (Nematollahi et al., 2020). General signs of neuronal death from AA exposure in humans include characteristic ataxia, weight loss, fragility of skeletal muscles, distal edema, and degeneracy of axons in the CNS and PNS (Rifai and Saleh, 2020). Epidemiological data suggest that long-term exposure to AA may raise the risk of endometrial hyperplasia, ovarian and breast cancer, and gynecologic cancer (Obón-Santacana et al., 2016; Adani et al., 2020). AA may negatively impact the male reproductive potential. Short-term exposure to high doses of AA may cause a variety of testicular toxicity in male rats, including reduced Leydig cell survivability, sperm abnormality, decreased concentration of epididymal sperm, altered sperm development and motility, germ cell degeneration, seminiferous tubule degeneration, production of multinucleated giant and apoptotic cells in seminiferous tubules, and decreased mating index (Peivasteh-Roudsari et al., 2024). Since AA has a low molecular weight and dissolves quickly in water, it may cross the placenta and be absorbed through breast milk in both humans and animals, which can have an impact on an infant's proper prenatal and early postnatal development (Kocadağlı and Gökmen, 2024). Because of AA's adverse effects on human health, it is crucial to determine its quantitative level.

High-pressure liquid chromatography (HPLC) in conjunction with mass spectrometry (MS) and gas chromatography (GC) are two popular technical methods used to detect AA (Omar et al., 2015; Abd El-Hady and Albishri, 2015). Despite being sensitive, these techniques have several drawbacks, including complicated sample pre-treatments, lengthy detection times, the need for costly equipment and skilled operators, and high analysis costs. Additionally, these methods fail to deliver analytical results within the allotted time (Pundir et al., 2019; Pan et al., 2020). Also, it is challenging to quantify AA using conventional techniques because of its low molecular weight (MW = 71.08 g/mol), high polarity and high volatility. Consequently, there is a growing need for quick, sensitive, affordable, and effective techniques to identify and measure AA amounts in dietary samples.

Biosensors have become increasingly common as a sensitive, quick, and economical means of detecting analytes. These devices combine a transducer and biological sensing element to transform the biochemical signal into an output that can be measured. The development of AA biosensors provides an effective way to track the amount of AA in various samples, including processed foods (Huang et al., 2016; Umam et al., 2017; Asnaashari et al., 2018) and environmental samples, by offering real-time, on-site detection capabilities and therefore help in mitigating health risks associated with AA exposure and contributing to a safer and healthier environment.

Numerous investigations have demonstrated that AA can form adducts with Hb ( Fig. 1) due to a chemical reaction involving the α-amine groups at the N-terminal amino acids in Hb (Pundir et al., 2019). However, the binding or interaction with the sulfhydryl (-SH) groups of the cysteine residues within Hb is usually linked to the selectivity. The AA-Hb adduct is formed when AA binds to residues Cys93 of the β-subunit and Cys95 of the α-subunit of human Hb. AA and related conjugated vinyl compounds perform Michael-type nucleophilic addition processes of –NH2 and –SH groups of amino acids, peptides, and proteins to their double bonds. Being a soft electrophile, AA combines with nucleophiles made up of cysteine, histidine, lysine, valine, and arginine residues found at the N-terminal regions of proteins (Favinha et al., 2020). Therefore, Hb can be used as a bioreceptor to identify AA. The direct adsorption of biomolecules such as Hb on bare electrode surfaces may lead to denaturation and loss of their bioactivity (Suprun, 2021). Instead, the adsorption of Hb onto the nanoparticles interface preserves its bioactivity because of the enhanced biocompatibility of nanoparticles. These nanoparticles provide flexible platforms that allow for the sensitive, targeted, and multiplexed detection of a wide range of analytes (Annisa et al., 2018).

The unique features of nanoparticles, including their large surface area, programmable surface chemistry, and remarkable optical, magnetic, or electronic capabilities, make them essential for biosensing applications. Nanocomposites are materials made up of a combination of nanoparticles and a matrix material, such as polymers, metals, or ceramics, and have demonstrated significant potential in biosensing applications. Incorporating nanocomposites into the biosensing platform can significantly enhance sensitivity due to the synergistic properties of the nanoparticles. The increased surface area-to-volume ratio of nanocomposite provides more sites for biomolecular recognition, leading to improved detection limits and signal amplification (Liu et al., 2016; Navarro et al., 2020).

The unique features, including high surface area, strong chemical stability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, good photocatalytic activity, and a broad range of applications of titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs), have attracted substantial attention in the sensing field (Çakıroğlu, and Özacar, 2020). They are used to increase the sensitivity and selectivity of sensors because of their large surface area and surface modification capabilities. In sensor systems, they detect biomolecules, gasses, and environmental contaminants (Garcia-Cabezon et al., 2015).

GQDs are zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with distinctive properties, including high surface area, excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, good aqueous solubility, high photostability against photobleaching, robust chemical inertness, tunable photoluminescence and easy preparation making them promising candidates for applications in medical diagnostics, biosensing, environmental monitoring, food safety, and point-of-care testing (Çakıroğlu, 2024). GQDs offer superior conductivity and high electron transport because of the quantum confinement and edge effect and act as a suitable transducer material for enhancing biosensor sensitivity (Campuzano et al., 2019).

Electrochemical sensing is a promising technique because of its higher sensitivity and selectivity, relative affordability, and ease of sample preparation for measurements. High conductivity and outstanding electrolytic performance of the chemically modified electrode are prerequisites for the electrochemical procedures. Pencil Graphite (PG) electrodes have drawn considerable interest because of their availability, miniaturization, biocompatibility, reproducibility, ease of modification, and portability (Torrinha et al., 2018).

AA detection by electrochemical sensing techniques is selective, highly sensitive, reproducible, and unaffected by other dietary constituents. Various kinds of electrochemical sensors were employed in different food matrices for the detection of AA (Uman et al., 2017; Asnaashari et al., 2019; Alam et al., 2021). However, the detection sensitivity of these sensors is not sufficient to determine trace levels of AA, thus limiting their utility. In addition, these previously developed sensors are costly and sophisticated to use (Wang et al., 2022).

In this study, we have designed and developed a highly sensitive and fast-responding electrochemical nanosensor for AA detection in processed foods. The nanocomposite of TiO2 NPs/GQDs was engineered to enhance the sensor's performance in terms of detection sensitivity, selectivity, and stability. Hb was covalently immobilized onto the modified electrode surface. Ultimately, the aim is to provide a reliable tool for monitoring AA levels in food products or environmental samples, enhancing food safety, and contributing to public health by mitigating exposure to AA.

Titanium (IV) isopropoxide (TTIP) (97 %) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, while citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O) was purchased from HIMEDIA. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and ethanol (C2H5OH) used in this study were of laboratory research (LR) grade, deionized water (DW), potassium ferricyanide, potassium ferrocyanide, and phosphate buffer were purchased from Merck Pvt. Ltd, sulphuric acid (H2SO4), silica, potassium hexacyanoferrate (II) trihydrate (Carrez I), EDC (1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide), zinc sulfate heptahydrate (Carrez II), Acrylamide (AA), NHS (N-hydroxysuccinimide), human hemoglobin (Hb, Sigma-Aldrich), and pencil graphite electrodes (Make; Apsara 8B), and different food products were taken from the local market.

Potentiostat/Galvanostat (Princton Applied Research, Parstat-4000) electrochemical cell with a three-electrode system having an auxiliary electrode (Pt), a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl), and a working electrode (PG), X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Smart Lab 3 kW, Rigaku) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker Alpha II), Potentiostat/Galvanostat Electrochemical impedance analyzer (EIS, PARASTAT), Scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Carl Zeiss), Transmission electron microscope (Tecnai, HR-TEM), UV- Visible Spectrophotometer (Malvern), ZETA-sizer/potential (Malvern Nano-ZS).

TiO2 NPs were synthesized using the sol-gel method (Dubey et al., 2019). First, ethanol (50 mL) was mixed with 4 mL of distilled water with constant stirring in a beaker; after that, 3 mL of TTIP was added dropwise while maintaining the solution temperature at 70–75ᵒC using a water bath. The solution was heated for 3–4 hrs until gel formation occurred. The gel was dried in a hot-air oven for 2 hrs at 60ᵒC. Finally, calcination was done in the furnace at 400ᵒC for 3 hrs to obtain TiO2 NPs. These NPs were characterized by TEM, ZETA analysis, UV–visible spectroscopy, FTIR spectroscopy, and XRD analysis.

GQDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid (Naik et al., 2017). Six grams of C6H8O7·H2O were taken in a glass beaker, heated, and melted at 60ᵒC for 20–25 minutes until a dark orange-coloured solution was obtained. Then, 1.5 M NaOH solution was prepared, added dropwise, and mixed with the above-prepared citric acid solution until the pH reached 9. TEM, Zeta analysis, FTIR, and UV–visible spectroscopy were used to characterize the obtained quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of TiO2 NPs/GQDs nanocomposite onto PG electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Pencil leads were purchased from the market. The polishing of the graphite electrode surface (2 cm × 2 mm) was carried out using silica slurry (diameter 0.05 μm) using a polishing cloth manually. Then, the electrode was washed with distilled water and treated with piranha solution (H2SO4: H2O2 in 3:1) to remove any organic residues bound to the electrode. The electrode was sonicated to remove unwanted particles adsorbed onto the PG electrode surface and washed with distilled water 2–3 times. TiO2 NPs/GQDs nanocomposite was prepared using ultrasonication (Shafaee et al., 2018). In a beaker, 20 mL of distilled water and 10 mL of ethanol were added to a mixture of 1 mL GQDs and 1 g of TiO2 NPs. The suspension was sonicated by an ultrasonic probe for 30 minutes at room temperature to obtain a homogeneous suspension. The mixture was centrifuged at 8500 x g for 10 minutes and washed with distilled water and ethanol. The resulting TiO2 NPs/GQDs nanocomposite was oven-dried and added (1 mg) to 50 mL of 1 M KCl, and electro-polymerization was carried onto the PG surface through CV using Potentiosatat-Galvanostat by applying 30 successive polymerization cycles at 0.1 to −0.1 V (Ensafi et al., 2015). The resulting nanocomposite-modified PG electrode was washed thoroughly with DW to remove unbound matter, characterized by SEM, FTIR spectroscopy, and electrochemical analysis, and stored at 4 °C.

The immobilisation of Hb onto TiO2 NPs/GQDs nanocomposite decorated PG electrode serves as the foundation for the fabrication of the AA nanosensor. Hb was covalently bonded onto the modified electrode to create the functioning electrode. First, the TiO2 NPs/GQDs nanocomposite-modified electrode was activated by immersing in 0.2 M of EDC-NHS (0.5 mL each) solution and kept for 2 hrs at room temperature. EDC-NHS activates the free-COOH groups of the TiO2 NPs/GQDs nanocomposite layer. The electrode was subsequently immersed in a 2 mL Hb solution, prepared in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0), and incubated for 24 hrs at 4ᵒC for immobilization. The -COOH groups of GQDs and the -NH2 groups on the Hb surface effectively collide during the incubation, forming amide (-CO-NH) bonds through EDC-NHS linkage (Muthurasu and Ganesh, 2014). Finally, the electrode was cleaned three or four times with 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) and used as a working electrode. CV was performed for the developed electrode at different scan rates of 10–120 mVs−1 to find the optimum current. The parameters affecting the working electrode's performance, including substrate (AA) concentration (10 nM to 240 nM), pH (4.0–6.0), temperature (20°C –50 °C), and incubation time (0–30 minutes) were optimized for AA sensing.

Different types of food samples, including potato chips, biscuits, fried nuts, and bread samples, each weighing 10 g, were crushed using a pestle and mortar and dissolved separately into 100 mL of DW. These mixtures were left for swelling for 1 hour at room temperature and then heated at 60ᵒC for another 1 hour with constant stirring. The mixtures were then centrifuged at 5500 rpm for 15 minutes. Hexane, 2 mL, was used to remove fat from the supernatants. Carrez I and Carrez II, each in a volume of 2 mL, were added to purify the resulting aqueous solutions, and the supernatants were filtered. AA concentrations were determined using the current nanosensor.

The electrodeposited Hb/TiO2 NPs/GQDs undergo passivation and reduced electron flow to the PG electrode because of Hb-AA adduct formation, which depends on the change in the electroactive properties of Hb in the presence of AA. Thus, more adduct formation occurs as AA concentration rises and current falls in direct proportion to AA concentration.

All the graphs and data analyses in the manuscript were generated using software OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA. Results were statistically analysed using regression analysis to evaluate the linearity and performance of the electrochemical nanosensor for AA detection. Standard deviations were calculated to assess the precision of the measurements. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were determined to establish the sensor's sensitivity. Values with P ≤ .05 were considered statistically significant.

Two different absorption peaks at 225 and 280 nm ( Fig. 2 A) were obtained in the UV–visible spectrum of GQDs. The 1st peak at 225 nm complies with the absorbance band of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons resulting from π–π* transitions in C = C groups, while the 2nd peak at 280 nm is associated with n–π* transitions of the C = O bonds (Khataee et al., 2017). The zeta potential showed a value of −20.4 (Fig. 2B), suggesting the high dispersion stability of GQDs. The FTIR spectrum of GQDs shows peaks around 1387. 71 cm−1, 3328.06 cm−1, 1015.88 cm−1, and 1641.54 cm−1 (Fig. 2 C) are attributed to C-N stretching, O-H stretching, C-O stretching, and CO stretching vibrations of the conjugated molecules. The peak at 1566.81 cm−1 is related to the CC bond vibration of the aromatic ring of GQDs. Meanwhile, the peak around 1111.27 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the C-O-C bond. The presence of hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of GQDs increases their solubility (Safardoust-Hojaghan and Salavati-Niasari, 2017). TEM images of GQD demonstrated an average size of < 50 nm diameter with spherical morphology (Fig. 2D).

Zeta potential measurement of TiO2 NPs showed the surface charges with a value of −21.4 mV ( Fig. 3 A), suggesting the high dispersion stability of TiO2 NPs (Purohit et al., 2020). The UV–visible spectra show an absorption peak around 350 nm (Fig. 3B), confirming the formation of TiO2 NPs. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3 C) showed a peak at 1062.44 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of CO groups, and the peak at 798.67 cm−1 and 901.76 cm−1 corresponds to the Ti-O-Ti stretching and bending vibrations of TiO2 NPs, respectively. The peak around 1540.75 cm−1 reflects the bending vibration of C-H functional groups. The band at 1254.40 cm−1 displays the alcohol functional groups. The peaks near 2983.00 cm−1 are assigned to alkane groups' C–H stretching vibrations. Other peaks observed at 3277.35 cm−1 and 3869.54 cm−1 are related to stretching hydroxyl (O-H) groups. The peak at 1650.43 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching of titanium carboxylate, formed from TTIP and ethanol as precursors (Thakur et al., 2019). TEM images of sol-gel derived nanoparticles (Fig. 3D) show spherical structures with an average diameter of 20 nm, enhancing the working electrode's surface area. The XRD pattern showed diffraction angle (2θ) at 25.25ᵒ, 37.98ᵒ, 48.02ᵒ, 54.08ᵒ, 62.71ᵒ, and 74.95ᵒ. The observed angle 2θ= 25.25ᵒ represents the high crystalline nature of TiO2 NPs. All the peaks in XRD patterns can be indexed as the anatase phase of TiO2 NPs (Haider et al., 2017).

The FTIR spectrum of the modified PG electrode showed characteristic peaks of TiO2 and GQD ( Fig. 4A). The 768.76 cm-1 and 907.91 cm-1 peaks correspond to the Ti-O-Ti stretching and bending vibrations. Meanwhile, the peak at 1517.85 cm−1 is related to the CC bond vibration of the aromatic ring of GQDs. The sharp peaks at 1692.40 cm−1 and 2980.84 cm−1 are attributed to CO and C–H stretching, respectively. Peaks observed between 3500.00 and 3800.00 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibrations of O-H groups. Moreover, the formation of broadbands and increase in the peak intensities in the modified PG electrode indicate the formation of Ti-O-C bonds between TiO2 and GQDs, indicating the chemisorption of GQDs and TiO2 NPs onto the PG surface (Ovchinnikov et al., 2016). Additional peaks are seen at 1060.86, 1398.90, and 1517.85 cm-1, which, in turn, indicate the existence of amine groups, stretching vibrations of the protein's C-N groups, and N-H stretch vibrations in the protein's amide II linkage, respectively (Fig. 4B). These peaks confirm the immobilization of Hb onto the electrode surface (Yadav et al., 2018). SEM images showed the smooth surface of the bare PG electrode (Fig. 4C), while TiO2 NPs/GQDs nanocomposites (Fig. 4D) and Hb/TiO2 NPs/GQDs (Fig. 4E) were immobilized onto the PG surface suggesting the excellent compatibility of the nanocomposite. EIS characterized the interfacial characteristics of the modified electrode. The real and imaginary components of the impedance are represented as Z′ and −Z″ by x- and y-axes, respectively. The EIS of the working electrode is shown by the Nyquist plot (Fig. 4F) in 5 mM K3Fe(CN)6:K4Fe(CN)6 (1:1). The electrodeposition of the nanocomposite and immobilisation of Hb was demonstrated by the changes in RCT values in the modified electrode. The decrease in RCT value in TiO2 NPs/GQDs/PG electrode indicates that the presence of nanocomposite enhanced electron transfer on the electrode surface due to their high electron transfer kinetics. However, after immobilising Hb, the RCT value increases because of the insulating properties of the biomolecule but is higher than the bare electrode due to the presence of nanocomposite (Yadav et al., 2018). CV of the bare PG electrode, nanocomposite-modified electrode, and Hb immobilized electrode was performed in 5 mM ferricyanide [K3Fe(CN)6] solution prepared in 1.0 M KCl, at −1 to + 1 V potential and a scan rate of 100 mV/s (Fig. 4G). Current decreased after immobilization of Hb because of the biomolecule (Hb) insulating properties (Asnaashari et al., 2019).

A Potentiostat/Galvanostat with three-electrodes system—a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl), an auxiliary electrode (Pt), and a working electrode (Hb/TiO2 NPs/GQDs/PG) was used to perform CV. The parameters affecting the working electrode's performance, including substrate concentration, pH, incubation time and temperature, were optimized for AA sensing.

The pH was adjusted from 4.5 to 8.0 at intervals of 0.5 using 0.1 M sodium acetate buffer (4.0–6.0 pH range), and 0.1 M sodium phosphate buffer (6.5–8.0 pH range). Optimum response was recorded at pH 7.0 ( Fig. 5 A). The effect of temperatures ranging from 20°C –50 °C on the developed electrode’s performance was studied. The optimum current was recorded at 30ᵒC (Fig. 5B). The CV of the modified electrode in the absence of AA was recorded in 0.1 M sodium acetate buffer. The addition of AA resulted in the formation of AA-Hb adduct, causing a decrease in peak current (Fig. 5 C) as a function of incubation time. An extended incubation period ranging from 0 to 30 minutes results in a notable reduction of the peak current value. Consequently, it was found that the optimal incubation time was 10 minutes. To remove the AA-Hb adduct from the TiO2 NPs/GQDs/PG surface, the working electrode was cleaned using CV in 0.1 M NaClO4 at a potential range from −2.0 to + 2.0 V (Wulandari et al., 2019). Standard solutions of different concentrations of AA (10 nM to 240 nM) were prepared in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0), and CV analysis was carried out. An inverse linear relationship was seen between the nanosensor response (Current) and AA concentration (Fig. 5D). The LOD and LOQ were calculated.

The nanosensor showed a linear response in the concentration range of 10 nM to 200 nM of AA. outside that, the current remained constant. Using the following formula, LOD = 3SM−1 and LOQ = 10SM−1, where S is the standard deviation of intercept, and M is the slope of the calibration curve, the LOD and LOQ were calculated to be 0.67 nM and 2.23 nM, respectively. Since the sample matrix contains a complex mixture of biomolecules, selectivity is a crucial parameter for any fabricated biosensor before it can be used in real applications. For this reason, the selectivity of Hb/TiO2 NPs/GQDs/PG nanosensor was assessed using potential interferents like propionic acid, ascorbic acid, acetate, tartrate, and acrylic acid ( Fig. 6 A). None of them demonstrated a discernible effect. As a result, the nanosensor's selectivity towards AA detection was rather high. By adding one unit of substrate concentration and observing the drop in current, sensitivity was calculated.
(1) Sensitivity = Slope (M)/ Active area of working electrode (A)
(Where, M= 0.002, A=
2 π rh +
π
r
2
= 0.835 cm2 (r = 0.05 cm & h= 2 cm), therefore sensitivity was 2.40 mAcm−2nM−1.

Analytical recoveries were investigated by introducing different AA concentrations (20,40,60, 80 and 100 nM). The relative standard deviation (RSD) values were less than 2.66 % ( Table 1). These results indicated that the RSD values less than 5 % exhibit high accuracy and reliability of the developed nanosensor. This nanosensor is more appropriate for quantitatively detecting AA in food samples. To ensure that the electrode was stable, the experiment was conducted once a week. After being repeated for 100 days, the sensor lost 15 % of its activity (Fig. 6B). This showed that the nanocomposite has good biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in processed foods
ce:section.ce:para: The nanosensor assessed the AA amount in processed foods (Table 3). To achieve this, 30 mL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.0), having 2 mL of food extract, was used to carry out CV analysis. AA content was determined to range from 93 nM to 168 nM. The results showed the variation in AA levels in processed foods, which is probably caused by processing techniques, ingredient composition, and cooking temperatures. Higher temperatures, longer thermal processing times, and higher amounts of reducing sugars and asparagine in raw materials are all linked to higher AA concentrations (Rifai and Saleh, 2020). More reducing sugars in potatoes result in more AA formation in potato chips. Nuts likely develop AA due to roasting processes that involve prolonged heat exposure. The low level of AA in bread and biscuits is due to less severe cooking conditions or lower precursor levels in the raw materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance and applications of the developed nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: In this research, a facile and effective nanosensor based on Hb/TiO2NPs/GQDs/PG electrode was developed for AA detection. The TiO2NPs/GQDs nanocomposite provides the working electrode with high electrical conductivity, stability, and biocompatibility. FTIR, UV–visible spectroscopy, TEM, XRD, and Zeta potential analysis were used to characterize the nanocomposite. Hb was Covalently immobilized onto a nanocomposite-modified PG electrode. Interaction of Hb with AA leads to the formation of Hb-AA adduct, which causes a decrease in current response. The working electrode was characterized at every stage of modification using FTIR and EIS measurements. The optimum response was observed at 30ᵒC in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). The nanosensor exhibits a detection limit of 0.67 nM, which is lower than many previously reported sensors as summarized in Table 2 (Huang et al., 2016; Umam et al., 2017). The high sensitivity of 2.40 mAcm−2nM−1 with the broad linear range of 10 nM to 200 nM of the nanosensor allows for better quantification of AA concentrations in various processed foods. The nanosensor also demonstrated excellent performance and storage stability for 100 days at 4ᵒC as compared to earlier reported AA sensors (Liu et al., 2016; Asnaashari et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020), as presented in Table 2. The enhanced stability of the current sensor makes it a viable option for long-term applications in food monitoring. AA was successfully detected in a concentration range of 93 nM to 168 nM in processed food samples, such as potato chips, biscuits, bread, and fried nuts, demonstrating its effectiveness in real food matrices. This versatility, combined with the improved performance metrics outlined in Table 2, highlights its potential for broader applications across different food industries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Safety guidelines for handling AA in the laboratory
ce:section.ce:para: Researcher safety when handling AA is crucial due to its hazardous nature, particularly its neurotoxic and potentially carcinogenic properties. To ensure safety, researchers must always wear proper personal protective equipment (PPE), including nitrile gloves, lab coats, and eye protection. As AA can be absorbed through the skin and inhaled, working in a well-ventilated area or under a fume hood is essential to prevent exposure to airborne particles. Avoiding direct skin contact and minimizing dust generation are key practices, especially when handling AA powder. Proper storage in tightly sealed, clearly labelled containers is also critical, and only the necessary quantity should be prepared for immediate use. All AA-contaminated waste, including gloves and pipette tips, should be disposed of as hazardous waste according to institutional protocols. The safety plan should also include medical monitoring, access to Safety Data Sheets (SDS), and regular safety training. These safety measures significantly reduce the risks associated with AA exposure.

It has drawn attention since AA interacts with biomolecules and negatively affects human health. We have fabricated a simple and effective electrochemical detection method for AA based on Hb/TiO2 NPs/GQDs/PG electrode. The developed nanosensor demonstrated impressive sensitivity, with a very low LOD of 0.67 nM and LOQ of 2.23 nM, coupled with a wide linear range of 10 nM to 200 nM and a high sensitivity of 2.40 mA cm⁻² nM⁻¹ . Overall, the developed nanosensor offers a promising approach for the rapid, sensitive, selective, stable, reusable, and robust detection of AA in processed foods and could be used to enhance food quality and safety standards. However, using biosensing technologies in food safety, healthcare, environmental monitoring, and other fields is still active research. Continued research and development in this field are expected to create even more advanced nanocomposite-based sensors with improved performance and broader applicability. These biosensors could be miniaturized and integrated into portable, point-of-care devices for rapid and on-site detection of analytes.

The authors are grateful to the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), New Delhi, India, for the financial support provided through the award of Senior Research Fellowship under grant number 09/0382(12481)/2021-EMR-I.

All authors contributed to the study’s conception and design. SA: Material preparation, data collection, conduction of experiment, and analysis, Writing, and plagiarism, Evaluation and editing, MS: Data validation and supervision. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.
Minakshi Sharma: Validation, Supervision. Shatrughan Aafria: Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
